title: ANSI/ASNT CP-189: The Employer-Based Standard
narrationScript: One word separates the two documents that govern employer-based certification. SNT-TC-1A says should. CP-189 says shall. In this lesson we take apart the standard, ANSI ASNT CP-189, and learn what changes when guidance hardens into minimum requirements. Start with what the document is. CP-189 is an American National Standard, balloted and published through the national standards process. Its scope is the qualification and certification of NDT personnel, and like the recommended practice it assumes an employer-based program. The employer still writes a written practice, and the employer still certifies. The difference is altitude. Where the recommended practice offers guidelines that the written practice may tailor with documented justification, the standard states minimum requirements. When a contract invokes CP-189, the employer's program must meet or exceed every shall in the document. The written practice can add, and it can tighten, but it cannot relax. An auditor working to the standard checks the program against the document itself, not merely against the company's own interpretation of it. The definitions carry the same firmness. Qualification remains the demonstrated combination of skill, knowledge, training, and experience. Certification remains written testimony that qualification has been achieved. But the standard goes further in defining who does what. It recognizes the familiar ladder, trainee, Level One, Level Two, Level Three, and it formally defines the NDT instructor, a person qualified and designated to deliver training, with documented credentials of their own. That matters to a Level Three building a program, because under the standard, training is not casual. It is delivered to a documented outline by people who are qualified to teach it. The sharpest difference sits at the top of the ladder. Under the standard, a candidate for employer certification as Level Three must first hold the central Level Three certificate from the national certifying body in that method. The employer then adds its own examination on company procedures and products before certifying. Think about what that does. It puts an independent, examination-based floor under every Level Three in every program that invokes the standard, so no employer can simply declare a favorite employee to be a Level Three without external evidence. Qualification requirements follow the same logic. The education, training, and experience tables in the standard are minimums, stated with shall, and the written practice carries them into company terms without reducing them. Vision requirements run on the clock you already know, near-vision acuity annually, and color discrimination at entry and at defined intervals. Examinations for Level One and Level Two remain general, specific, and practical, administered under the responsibility of the Level Three, and the standard states its grading rule plainly, a composite of at least eighty percent with no individual examination below seventy. Annual technical performance evaluations of working Level Ones and Level Twos are required, documented looks at real performance, not paperwork formalities. Because requirements are firm, the standard also spells out what happens when things go wrong. Certification expires when its interval runs out, and employer recertification happens at intervals not exceeding five years under the standard's model. Certification is suspended when a condition lapses, an overdue vision examination, an unsatisfactory performance evaluation, and suspension ends when the cause is corrected. Certification is revoked for cause, falsified records, ethical violations, gross incompetence, and revocation is not cured by apology. Reinstatement after revocation, or after a long gap, generally means satisfying the requirements again, the way an initial candidate would. Keep those three words straight, expired, suspended, revoked, because they have different causes and different cures, and an exam question will turn on the difference. Finally, records. The standard requires the employer to maintain the evidence. The written practice itself. Each person's education, training, and experience documentation. Examination records with dates, grades, and the identity of who administered them. Vision examination results. Performance evaluations. The certifications with their dates and signatures. Records must be retained per the program and be available for audit, because under a standard, the record is the proof that the shall was met. And like the recommended practice, the standard names its companion publications, the topical outlines that define the training body of knowledge and the central certification program it builds on. Hold the comparison in one line. The recommended practice bends to fit the employer. The standard makes the employer rise to fit it. Know which document a contract invokes before you write a single word of program paperwork, because that one fact changes what every sentence of your written practice is allowed to say.
imagePrompt: Two quality engineers compare thick requirements documents side by side at a desk in a glass-walled office overlooking an aerospace machining floor with CNC equipment below.

sections.heading: Shall Means Shall: Scope and Definitions
sections.body:
ANSI/ASNT CP-189 is an **American National Standard** for the qualification and certification of NDT personnel — balloted through the consensus standards process and written as **minimum requirements** with the verb **shall**. Its scope mirrors the territory of SNT-TC-1A: an **employer-based** program in which the employer maintains a written practice and certifies its own people. What changes is the force of the text. When a contract or specification invokes CP-189, the employer's program must **meet or exceed every requirement** in it. The written practice may add and may tighten; it may **not relax**. There is no 'modify with justification' escape hatch for the standard's minimums.

The definitions keep the backbone you already know — **qualification** is the demonstrated combination of skill, knowledge, training, and experience; **certification** is written testimony that qualification was achieved — but the standard sharpens the cast of characters. It defines the **NDT personnel levels**, the **employer** as certifying body, and, notably, the **NDT Instructor**: a formally qualified and designated person responsible for delivering training to a documented outline. Under the standard, who teaches is itself a controlled variable.

For exam purposes, anchor the contrast: *recommended practice = adjustable guidance made binding by the written practice; CP-189 = binding minimums the written practice must carry without reduction.*

sections.caption: CP-189 qualification levels and defined roles, including the NDT Instructor and the central-certificate prerequisite feeding employer Level III certification.

sections.heading: Levels of Qualification — and the Level III Prerequisite
sections.body:
The ladder under CP-189 is familiar: **trainee, Level I, Level II, Level III**, with responsibilities matching the model you learned in the first lesson — Level I performs to written instructions, Level II sets up, interprets, and evaluates to acceptance criteria, Level III establishes procedures, interprets codes, and runs the technical program. The standard also recognizes **limited certifications** with appropriately reduced requirements where scope is narrowed, and it formalizes the **NDT Instructor** as a defined, documented role in the training system.

The sharpest single difference from the recommended practice sits at the top. To be certified Level III by the employer under the standard, the candidate **shall hold the central ASNT NDT Level III certificate in the method** as the examination basis; the employer then administers its own **specific examination** on company procedures, products, and applicable codes before certifying. The effect is structural: an independent, third-party examination floor under every Level III in every CP-189 program. An employer working purely to SNT-TC-1A *may* examine its own Level III candidates in-house (or accept the ASNT certificate in lieu); an employer working to CP-189 has no such latitude.

This is a favorite Basic-exam discriminator: who can become a Level III, and on whose examination evidence, under each document.

sections.heading: Qualification Requirements, Certification, and Examinations
sections.body:
**Qualification requirements.** The standard's education, training, and experience tables are **minimums**. The written practice translates them into company terms — methods, levels, job titles — but cannot cut hours or months below the standard. Credit pathways the standard itself provides (for example, recognizing engineering or science coursework) are the only permitted reductions. Vision requirements follow the familiar clock: **near-vision acuity annually**, and **color and gray-shade discrimination at entry and at defined intervals**.

**Qualification and certification.** As always, qualification precedes certification: the employer verifies education, training, and experience, administers examinations, verifies vision, and only then issues the certification — written testimony, signed and dated, traceable to the evidence.

**Examinations.** Level I and Level II candidates take **general, specific, and practical** examinations administered under the responsibility of the Level III (the standard is explicit about examiner duties and about documenting who administered and graded). The standard states its grading rule plainly: a **composite of at least 80 percent with no individual examination grade below 70 percent**. Reexamination of failed candidates follows defined waiting/retraining provisions. Working Level I and Level II personnel also receive **annual technical performance evaluations** — documented observation of real work, with unsatisfactory results carrying consequences for the certification.

sections.caption: CP-189 and SNT-TC-1A at a glance — the contrast the Basic exam loves to probe.
rows: Guidelines published as a recommended practice
May add or tighten; may not relax minimums
May modify with documented justification in the written practice
Central ASNT Level III certificate required, plus employer specific examination
Employer's own examinations; central certificate may be accepted in lieu
Training roles handled through the written practice
Composite ≥ 80%, no examination < 70% (stated in the standard)
Composite ≥ 80% is the common model; per-exam floors set by the written practice
The standard itself, plus the written practice carrying it

sections.title: One verb changes the whole program
sections.body: Before drafting or auditing any written practice, confirm **which document the contract invokes**. Under SNT-TC-1A you may tailor with documented justification; under **CP-189 every 'shall' is a floor** you must meet or exceed. The same paragraph of program text can be compliant under one document and a finding under the other.

sections.caption: Certification states under the standard: expiration, suspension, and revocation have different causes and different cures.

sections.heading: Expiration, Suspension, Revocation, Reinstatement — and Recertification
sections.body:
Because its requirements are firm, the standard is explicit about the ways a certification stops being valid, and the three states are not interchangeable.

- **Expired** — the certification interval ran out. Cure: **employer recertification**, at intervals **not exceeding five years** under the standard's model, by the examination or documented-performance evidence the standard and written practice require.
- **Suspended** — a standing condition lapsed while the certification was otherwise current: an overdue vision examination, an unsatisfactory annual performance evaluation, interrupted service beyond the program's limits. Cure: **correct the cause** — pass the vision test, complete retraining and reevaluation — and the certification is restored without full requalification.
- **Revoked** — pulled **for cause**: falsified records, violation of the program's ethics requirements, demonstrated incompetence. Cure: there is no shortcut — **reinstatement after revocation generally means satisfying the requirements as an initial candidate would**, at the employer's discretion and per the written practice.

Certification also ends with **employment termination**, exactly as under the recommended practice, with reinstatement provisions for timely rehires. On the Basic exam, expect scenario questions that hinge on choosing the right state — and therefore the right cure.

sections.heading: Records and Referenced Publications
sections.body:
Under a standard, the record **is** the compliance. CP-189 requires the employer to establish and maintain the documentation that proves every 'shall' was met, and to keep it available for audit:

- the current **written practice** itself
- each individual's **education, training, and experience** evidence
- **examination records** — dates, grades, content identification, and who administered and graded
- **vision examination** results and dates
- **technical performance evaluations**
- the **certifications**: level, method, dates of issue and expiration, and the certifying signature

Retention periods and control of these records follow the program's quality system; the practical test is whether an auditor can reconstruct any certification, end to end, from the file alone.

The standard closes with its **referenced publications** — the companion documents it builds on, principally the ASNT **topical outlines** (the body-of-knowledge documents this course follows) and the **central Level III certification program** that supplies the examination basis for employer Level III certification. As with the recommended practice, a disciplined written practice cites the editions it works to, because requirements shift between editions and audits key to the cited one.

interactive.title: Which Document Says That?
interactive.intro: A contract review lands eight program statements on your desk. Classify each one: does it describe ANSI/ASNT CP-189, the standard — or SNT-TC-1A, the recommended practice?

items.label: Written as minimum requirements using 'shall'
items.why: CP-189 is an American National Standard: its requirements are binding minimums the employer must meet or exceed.

items.label: Guidelines the employer may modify with documented technical justification
items.why: SNT-TC-1A expressly anticipates tailoring — the written practice documents and justifies departures from its guidelines.

items.label: Level III certification built on a required central ASNT Level III certificate plus an employer specific examination
items.why: The standard requires the central certificate as the Level III examination basis; the employer adds its own specific examination.

items.label: Formally defines and qualifies the NDT Instructor role
items.why: CP-189 treats who delivers training as a controlled, documented role with its own qualification requirements.

items.label: Its recommendations bind only as adopted into the employer's written practice
items.why: The recommended practice has no force of its own — the written practice (and the contract invoking it) creates the obligation.

items.label: Training-hour and experience tables are starting points the employer tailors
items.why: Under SNT-TC-1A the tables are suggested values; under the standard the equivalent tables are non-reducible minimums.

items.label: Balloted and published as an American National Standard
items.why: CP-189 carries ANSI consensus-standard status; SNT-TC-1A is published as a recommended practice, not a standard.

items.label: Leaves per-examination minimum grades for the written practice to set
items.why: The recommended practice's common model is a composite of at least 80 with floors chosen by the written practice; the standard fixes composite ≥ 80 with no examination below 70.

checkQuestions.question: The fundamental difference between CP-189 and SNT-TC-1A is that CP-189:
options: Applies only to aerospace work while SNT-TC-1A applies everywhere else
Is a central certification program run by ASNT rather than the employer
States binding minimum requirements ('shall') that the employer may not relax, while SNT-TC-1A offers guidelines the employer may tailor
Eliminates the need for an employer written practice
checkQuestions.explanation: Both documents serve employer-based programs with a written practice — but CP-189 is an American National Standard whose minimums must be met or exceeded, while SNT-TC-1A is a recommended practice the employer adapts with documented justification.

checkQuestions.question: Under CP-189, an employer certifying a new Level III in ultrasonic testing must base that certification on:
options: Any documented twenty years of UT experience
A current central ASNT NDT Level III certificate in UT plus the employer's specific examination
The employer's general, specific, and practical examinations alone
A recommendation letter from the previous employer's Level III
checkQuestions.explanation: The standard requires the central ASNT Level III certificate as the examination basis for employer Level III certification, supplemented by the employer's own specific examination on its procedures and products.

checkQuestions.question: A Level II's annual technical performance evaluation is unsatisfactory, and separately a falsified inspection record is discovered for another technician. Under the standard's model, the appropriate certification actions are, respectively:
options: Revoke both certifications permanently
Suspend pending retraining and reevaluation for the first; revoke for cause for the second
Let both certifications expire naturally at the next interval
Suspend both until the next scheduled recertification examination
checkQuestions.explanation: An unsatisfactory evaluation is a lapsed condition — suspension cured by correcting the cause. Falsification is cause for revocation, after which reinstatement generally means requalifying as an initial candidate.

checkQuestions.question: Which record set would an auditor expect to reconstruct any CP-189 certification from the file alone?
options: The certificate wallet card and a current resume
Written practice; education, training, and experience evidence; examination records with grades and examiner identity; vision results; performance evaluations; signed certifications with dates
Production test reports signed by the technician over the past year
The purchase orders for the jobs the technician worked
checkQuestions.explanation: Under a standard, records are the proof of compliance: the program document plus per-person qualification evidence, examination and vision records, evaluations, and the signed, dated certifications.

checkQuestions.question: Under the standard's model, employer recertification of NDT personnel occurs at intervals:
options: Not exceeding five years, supported by mechanisms such as annual technical performance evaluations between examinations
Not exceeding ten years for all levels
Only when a customer contract demands it
Annually, by repeating all three qualification examinations
checkQuestions.explanation: The standard's model sets a maximum recertification interval of five years, with annual technical performance evaluations (and vision checks) maintaining the certification's validity between recertifications.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-189, Standard for Qualification and Certification of Nondestructive Testing Personnel (requirements summarized, current edition)
